TextENGNew: My name is Satoko Houjou.
But 'Houjou' is my current father's family name.
When I had different fathers, I was Satoko Hatake, Satoko Yoshizawa, and Satoko Matsuura.
changeReason: New adds causal context from the Japanese: 'When I had different fathers' explicitly explains WHY the names changed, which the original omits.

TextENGNew: So when someone calls me 'Houjou-san,' it still doesn't feel like it belongs to me.
changeReason: New translation adds 'it still doesn't feel like it belongs to me' from the Japanese しっくり来ません, conveying a sense of alienation beyond mere unfamiliarity.

TextENGNew: If they call me Satoko, I can tell right away they mean me.
Because Satoko is the only name I've been called since the day I was born.
changeReason: New adds 'only' (matching Japanese 唯一 'sole/only'), emphasizing uniqueness that the original missed.

TextENGNew: I don't understand why my mother keeps getting divorced and remarried.
If she's going to get divorced, why get married?
If she's going to get married, why get divorced?
I've asked her about it several times, but her answers changed depending on the circumstances.
When I asked while she was married, she got angry.
When I asked after a divorce, she cried.
I don't understand what marriage means.
So I have no interest in the brides and wedding dresses the other girls my age draw with their crayons.
changeReason: Original implies Satoko herself would draw; new correctly says she has no interest in what OTHER girls her age draw with crayons, matching the Japanese.

TextENGNew: I don't think I'll ever get married.
I don't want to keep changing my child's last name and confusing them, so I don't want to get married.
changeReason: New translation adds the full reasoning from the Japanese: 'confusing them' and the conclusion 'so I don't want to get married', which the original truncated.

TextENGNew: A teacher once asked me if I liked my mother.
I think it was during kindergarten in Okinomiya.
That was a very difficult question.
Because sometimes I loved her dearly and sometimes I hated her intensely—I couldn't say which it was.
When my mother was kind, she was always smiling.
She made our family fun and lively.
changeReason: New translation specifies 'our family fun and lively' matching the Japanese 家族を楽しく盛り上げて (made family fun and lively), while original just says 'She made us happy'.

TextENGNew: She told me, 'Thank you for being born.'
changeReason: Original 'thanked me for being her daughter' differs from the Japanese 生まれてきてありがとう meaning 'thank you for being born'; the new translation correctly captures this.

TextENGNew: When my mother wasn't kind, she was always irritable.
changeReason: Original says 'frowned all the time' but the Japanese says イライラ (irritable/frustrated); new correctly translates this emotional state.

TextENGNew: She was cruel—so cruel—to my brother and me.
changeReason: New captures the Japanese repetition 辛く辛く (cruel, so cruel) with emphatic repetition that the original's 'very poorly' misses.

TextENGNew: She told me, 'I wish I'd never had you.'
changeReason: Old says 'I should have never been born' (Satoko internalizing); new says 'I wish I'd never had you' (quoting the mother). Both convey birth regret but differ in attribution/voice.

TextENGNew: The teacher also asked me if I liked my father.
changeReason: New adds 'also' matching the Japanese とも, indicating this question followed the previous one; the original omits this connection.

TextENGNew: That was a very easy question.
Because my father was only ever someone I hated intensely. No matter which father it was.
changeReason: New clarifies 'No matter which father it was' matching the Japanese 大嫌いな時と大嫌いな時しかなくて (only times of intense hatred), while original's 'No matter who he was' is vaguer.

TextENGNew: A newly married father was always smiling.
changeReason: Original uses plural 'fathers' generalizing across multiple stepfathers; new uses singular 'father' matching the Japanese singular perspective of each individual new father.

TextENGNew: He would dote on me as though I were his real daughter.
He would tell me, 'You can think of me as your real father.'
But he could never be my real father.
In the first place, I didn't even know my real father's face.
When a stranger like that asks me to call him 'Father,' I can't do it.
changeReason: New adds 'a stranger like that' matching the Japanese そんな人 (such a person/stranger), providing context the original omits.

TextENGNew: It's just revolting.
Right before a divorce, my father was always irritable.
He treated me like a cockroach.
He said horrible things to me and flew into a rage over the slightest displeasure.
changeReason: Old fabricates specific actions ('no money for food, threw things, locked on veranda') not in JPN, which says 'said horrible things and flew into a rage over the slightest displeasure.'

TextENGNew: A teacher told me I could forget about all that because it was in the past.
I think it was a different teacher at a different school.
Maybe she was right.
Because ever since Houjou became my father, my mother has been smiling.
The kind of bitter fighting that always preceded a divorce hasn't happened.
...They do quarrel sometimes, but for some reason it never leads to divorce—they always make up.
But I'm sure the topic of divorce will come up sooner or later.
Then it'll be the same as always—money, where to live next... those conversations will start right away.
changeReason: Old fabricates 'family court, divorce settlements, a night flight, and child support' not in JPN, which simply says 'money, where to live next.'

TextENGNew: So I don't know how long this Houjou father will last, and I have no desire to call him Father.
At first, this father also treated me well.
But no matter how many times he asked me to call him Father, I wouldn't, and he grew more and more irritated.
Divorce will come soon.
When a father starts acting this way, divorce is on the horizon.
After the divorce, it will be my mother's turn to be irritable.
And then she'll tell me she should never have had me, or that she can't be happy because of me.
Why does my very existence cause problems for my mother?
I asked my brother, and he explained it to me.
Apparently, having children makes it harder to remarry.
Since remarrying is such important work for my mother, I can understand how having children around would be difficult.
I think I finally understand, just a little, why I'm a nuisance to her.
changeReason: New adds 'just a little' from the Japanese 少しだけ, conveying Satoko's partial understanding that the original omits.

TextENGNew: So I asked my brother something else.
Why are children hated by new fathers?
That was simple too.
It's because family members have different roles.
A mother's role is to love her children.
A father's role is to love the mother.
Which means I am a nuisance to both my mother and my father.
My brother is much more mature than I am, so they don't treat him like as much of a bother.
But me—I am certainly, definitely, probably, no... absolutely a nuisance.
changeReason: New captures the Japanese's deliberate escalation きっと絶対、多分それでも、いや必ず (certainly, definitely, probably even so, no, absolutely), while original simplifies to just two stages.

TextENGNew: So now I understand.
Both my mother and my father wish I were dead.
But if they killed me, the police would catch them.
That doesn't mean they'd never try.
If I happened to be peering over the edge of a cliff,
and no one was watching,
my mother and father would surely push me off.
My father has become strangely, disgustingly nice to me lately.
changeReason: New adds 'disgustingly' matching the Japanese 気持ち悪いくらいに (to the point of being creepy/disgusting), which 'abnormally' doesn't capture.

TextENGNew: He started acting that way right after I made that fake phone call.
It must be a strategy to lower my guard so he can kill me.
changeReason: New adds 'to lower my guard' from the Japanese 油断させよう (make me let my guard down), specifying the strategy that the original omits.

TextENGNew: So I'm going to make sure I'm never fooled.
...I'm sorry,@|@y I'm sorry,@|@y I'm sorry,@|@y I'm sorry...
Lately, if I listen very carefully, I can hear a voice that keeps apologizing.
Whoever it is must be just like me—told by their mother that they shouldn't have been born.
changeReason: Original adds 'The voice sounds similar to mine' which is not in the Japanese; new correctly translates 'must be just like me' without the invented detail.

TextENGNew: ...I'm sorry,@|@y I'm sorry,@|@y I'm sorry,@|@y I'm sorry...
But I won't apologize like that.
I will survive.
I will fight back and win.
changeReason: New adds 'and win' from the Japanese 返り討ちにしてやっつける (fight back and defeat them), while original only says 'strike back'.

TextENGNew: I will kill before I am killed.
...I'm sorry,@|@y I'm sorry,@|@y I'm sorry,@|@y I'm sorry...
